text: The surgeon mobilizes the right flexure of the colon using sharp dissection and cautery. The caulker maneuver is initiated, reflecting the duodenum medially, with the kidney visible in the background.
The surgeon begins dividing the gastrocolic ligament using energy devices, progressing towards the gastroduodenal junction. Part of the pancreas gland is visible on the screen.

The surgeon identifies the gastroepiploic vessels and the gastrocolic trunk. Small attachments of the posterior stomach are divided, making the gland more visible. The gastroepiploic artery and vein are double clipped and divided.

The surgeon navigates around the gastroduodenal junction, using umbilical tape to assist in firing staples distally to the pylorus. The dissection of the gastroduodenal artery is shown, with the artery being divided between clips. The dissection extends to the hepatic hilum, with the portal vein visible in the background.

The surgeon carefully develops a channel between the superior mesenteric vein, the portal vein, and the gland using cautery. The head of the pancreas is divided from the body, followed by the dissection of the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein from the pancreatic head. The intestine is divided using a mechanical stapler from the right side of the patient.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the small bowel mesentery, followed by the division of the space between the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and superior mesenteric artery using cautery and sharp dissection. The common bile duct is divided proximally to the cystic duct insertion, and the surrounding tissue is resected.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy, passing proline stitches through the pancreas and intestine. The intestine is opened at the anti-mesenteric border, and a ductal mucosa anastomosis is created using a running suture with 5-0 PDS. A stent is placed across the anastomosis to support it and prevent leaks.
The surgeon proceeds to the choledochojejunostomy, performing the anastomosis using a running suture with 5-0 PDS, without placing a stent.

The surgeon prepares to perform the final anastomosis between the stump of the duodenum and the jejunum, executing a two-layer technique similar to an open procedure. The completed anastomosis is visible.
